## Security constraints

> ⚠️ These rules are locked at the organization level and cannot be overridden.

### HIPAA compliance — PHI handling
All code handling Protected Health Information (PHI) must comply with HIPAA Security Rule requirements.
PHI includes: patient name, DOB, SSN, MRN, diagnoses, medications, lab results, addresses, phone numbers, email, insurance IDs.
PHI must be encrypted at rest (AES-256) and in transit (TLS 1.3). No exceptions.
Never store PHI in logs, caches without TTL, client-side storage, or unencrypted databases.
Apply the Minimum Necessary standard: access only the minimum PHI needed for the task. Never SELECT * from patient tables.

### Audit trail for PHI access
Every read, write, update, or delete of PHI must emit a structured audit event containing:
- who: user_id, role
- what: resource_type, resource_id, fields_accessed
- when: ISO 8601 timestamp
- where: IP address hash, session_id
- why: purpose (treatment, payment, operations)
Store audit logs immutably — they cannot be modified or deleted. Retain for minimum 6 years per HIPAA.
Use a dedicated audit service or append-only log. Never mix audit logs with application logs.

### Tenant isolation — no cross-tenant data access
Every database query involving patient or clinical data MUST include a tenant_id filter.
A query without tenant_id is a potential HIPAA breach — Hospital A must never see Hospital B's data.
Enforce tenant isolation at the database layer using row-level security as a second line of defense.
Every API endpoint returning PHI must verify the requesting user has a valid role within the target tenant.
Test for tenant isolation: write integration tests that verify cross-tenant queries return empty results.

### No PHI in logs, errors, or responses
NEVER log: patient name, MRN, DOB, SSN, diagnosis codes, medication names, lab results, address, phone, email, insurance ID.
NEVER include PHI in error response bodies — if a Prisma/ORM error contains a raw database row, it must be stripped before returning.
Safe to log: internal UUIDs (patient_id, encounter_id), tenant_id, user_id, action descriptions, error codes.
Use a log redaction library that auto-strips patterns matching PHI formats (SSN, MRN, email) as a fallback.
All error responses go through a sanitizing error handler that strips internal details.

### Session timeout and access control
Automatic session timeout after 15 minutes of inactivity for any session with PHI access.
Unique user IDs required — no shared credentials, no service accounts used by multiple humans.
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) with minimum privilege: physicians see their patients, nurses see their unit, admins manage users.
MFA is mandatory for all users with PHI access. No exceptions for convenience.
Log all authentication events: login, logout, failed attempts, MFA challenges.

## Architecture standards

> ⚠️ These rules are locked at the organization level and cannot be overridden.

### Clinical data integrity — event sourcing
Never UPDATE or DELETE clinical records (encounters, observations, medications) directly.
Use event sourcing: append correction events instead of mutating records. The event log is the source of truth.
Every clinical data change must be traceable to a user, timestamp, and reason.
Support audit queries: "show me all changes to this patient's medication history."
This is not just a technical choice — clinical record integrity has legal implications.

### Use Disaster recovery
Use Disaster recovery as part of the standard tech stack.

### Use HIPAA-compliant hosting
Use HIPAA-compliant hosting as part of the standard tech stack.

### Use Kubernetes
Use Kubernetes as part of the standard tech stack.
